notes: lcstr/ucstr/capstr/strlen all have nargs=-1 in functions.c's table -- the only negative value used anywhere in that table -- meaning parse_arglist does NOT comma-split their argument at all: the whole parenthesized text is evaluated as a single expression (commas and all, literal except where they still happen to parse as nested constructs). This isn't a special no-eval rule -- lcstr(add(1,2)) (no comma) evaluates normally to 3 -- it falls out of jmush's already-correct one-shot-EV_FCHECK-per-scan behavior: scanning "a,add(1,2)" as one blob, the name candidate at the first ( is "a,add" (not a real function), so it falls back to literal and permanently burns that scan's one-shot budget, meaning add(1,2)'s own ( can never fire either. (Forced-eval ([...]) inherits EV_FMAND into the nested argument evaluation, turning a failed name match into a hard error instead of a literal fallback -- oracle-confirmed [lcstr(HELLO,add(1,2))] gives '#-1 function (hello,add) not found', but EV_FMAND itself is a pre-existing, separately-tracked gap (see EvalFlags's javadoc, "not modeled... needs the Value error-message system"), so no corpus case here covers it.) The dynamic-function-name path ([setq(0,lcstr)]%q0(...)) resolves identically to the static case -- oracle-verified -- since real TinyMUSH has no static/dynamic split in its name-resolution mechanism.
